coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Ocimum sanctum for effective fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions and live cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Chowdhuri, Angshuman Ray
Laha, Dipranjan
Mahto, Triveni Kumar
Karmakar, Parimal
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A simple, rapid and cost-effective approach is developed to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using the leaves of Ocimum sanctum as a carbon source for the first time. The as-synthesized CDs possess high stability in aqueous solution and exhibit strong fluorescence with quantum yield of 9.3%. We have explored the use of such CDs as a fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ ions detection, which is based on Pb2+ ions induced fluorescence quenching of CDs. More significantly the resultant CDs has excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Pb2+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.59nM and linear detection range of 0.01–1.0μM. The practical use of synthesized CDs for detection of Pb2+ ions is demonstrated in triple negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB 468 cells) and real water samples successfully. Moreover, the CDs are also possessing low cytotoxicity to exhibit excellent fluorescent probe for multicolour cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Carbon dots
Multi colour cell imaging
Pb2+ ions sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence of CDs in different pH
Surface morphology analysis and particles size determination
Detection of lead (II) ions in real water samples
Cytotoxicity of CDs through MTT assay
MDA-MB 468 cell imaging by synthesized CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Rajasthan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the DST, Government of India (SB/FT/CS-068/2013) and Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of green synthetic procedure of CDs for Pb2+ ions detections.

(a): Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths progressively decreases from 450 to 530nm. (b) UV–vis spectra of CDs [inset: photoluminescence spectra of CDs and photographic images of CDs under (a) Visible light, (b) UV light, and (c) UV light with addition of lead ions (10μM)].
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with addition of Pb2+ ions solution (0.01–10μM) and (b) bar diagram of different metal ions sensing (inset: visual detection of Pb2+ ions).

Fluorescence images of visual detection of trace Pb2+ ions. Decreases of the fluorescence intensity after addition of the different concentration lead ions, and fluorescence photos taken under UV lamp with an excitation wavelength at 365nm.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of CDs.
(a) HRTEM image (b) particles size distribution calculated from HRTEM image by using Image J (c) FESEM image and (d) EDX spectrum of CDs.
The fluorescence detection of Pb2+ ions in real water samples at different concentrations of various water sources [(a) mineral water, (b) tap water, and (c) pond water] by CDs.

Fluorescence microscopy image of MDA-MB 468 cells incubated with CDs [(a) bright-field, and (c, e, and g) at 330–385nm, 450−480nm, and 510–550nm excitation wavelengths] and (b, d, f and h) represents the corresponding fluorescence quenching after addition of lead ions (10μM).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Ocimum sanctum for effective fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions and live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, 188, Raja S C Mallick Road, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, rapid and cost-effective approach is developed to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using the leaves of Ocimum sanctum as a carbon source for the first time. The as-synthesized CDs possess high stability in aqueous solution and exhibit strong fluorescence with quantum yield of 9.3%. We have explored the use of such CDs as a fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ ions detection, which is based on Pb2+ ions induced fluorescence quenching of CDs. More significantly the resultant CDs has excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Pb2+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.59nM and linear detection range of 0.01–1.0μM. The practical use of synthesized CDs for detection of Pb2+ ions is demonstrated in triple negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB 468 cells) and real water samples successfully. Moreover, the CDs are also possessing low cytotoxicity to exhibit excellent fluorescent probe for multicolour cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic heavy metal ion pollution, as a serious environmental issue has received growing attention in worldwide because of their latent toxic effects toward human health and animals. Among toxic heavy metals, lead is considered as one of the most hazardous heavy metal ions in the environment. Lead ions can cause serious problems such as brain damage [1], neurological/cardiovascular disorders and mental retardation [2]. In recent years, the detection of lead ions has encouraged the improvement of new techniques with high selectivity and sensitivity. Recently, some analytical techniques have been employed for the lead detection, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [3–5] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [6–8]. However, these techniques often require sophisticated and expensive instrumentation which hinders their practical application for lead ion detection. Recently, fluorescence method has been enabled for the development of new detection process because of their more sensitive and faster detection of lead ions in aqueous solution [9]. Fluorescence methods are extremely attractive because of its cost-effectiveness, ease of operation, simplicity and high sensitivity [10]. In recent years, various DNAzyme sensor [11], aptamer [12], polymer dots [13] and inorganic nanomaterials like gold [14], CdS [15], ZnS [16] have been developed to monitor and analyse the Pb2+ ions by the fluorescence method. Simultaneously, some researchers have developed some liposomes and magnetic nanoparticles for Pb2+ ions sensing [17]. All above reported sensors possess advantages, like simple treatment process, high sensitivity, and short analysis time [10]. But unfortunately, use of DNAzyme, aptamer and gold nanoparticles is limited for the routine analysis due to cost effectiveness [11,12,14]. Application of inorganic quantum dots is also imperfect due to the effect of toxicity caused by heavy metals, while liposome and magnetic nanoparticles suffer from multistep synthetic approach [17]. Therefore, it is highly demanded to synthesize a simple and inexpensive materials to apply as excellent selective and sensitive probes for Pb2+ ions detection.

Recently, fluorescent CDs have received increasing interest in research owing to their potential applications in areas ranging from imaging, sensing [13] and drug delivery [18] to optoelectronic devices. Compared to the conventional organic fluorescent dyes and heavy metal based quantum dots, CDs have created great focus because of their facile synthesis, splendid water solubility, low-toxicity, high photostability, low cost and excellent biocompatibility [19–22]. In particular, based on their robust and tuneable photoluminescence properties, CDs were effectively employed in the detection of heavy metal ions [23]. In the past two years, few groups proposed fluorescence methods based on CDs for the assay of lead ions [24]. Dong et al. have used Graphene Quantum Dots/L-Cysteine electrochemiluminescence system for the selective and sensitive detection of lead (II) ions. [9] Jiang et al. synthesized N-doped carbon dots using glycerol as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen doped molecule through microwave irradiation method for Pb2+ detection [25]. Wee et al. reported the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin for sensitive sensing probe for lead (II) ions [26]. Wang et al. designed carbon dots a fluorescent probe for the sensitive determination of Pb2+ ions in an aqueous solution. Although these CDs-based materials hold good potential in the detection of lead ions in aqueous solution, however, each of these reported CDs has its own limitations like complex synthetic procedure or time-consuming functionalization.

Significant investigation is still needed to explore the potential of these CDs in the development of advanced sensors for Pb2+ ions detection. In our case, we have synthesized CDs in a single step using natural resource with a green approach for lead ions sensing with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.59nM.

CDs have been synthesized using a wide variety of techniques such as acidic oxidation [27], electrochemical synthesis [28], hydrothermal synthesis [29], laser ablation [30], arc discharge [31], and microwave-assisted synthesis [9]. Among them, hydrothermal methods using cheap and eco-friendly biomass such as apple juice [32], orange juice [33], sweet potatoes [34], garlic [35], honey [36], shrimps [37] and alovera [20] as the carbon sources to synthesize CDs have encouraged great interest because of their green chemistry approach. It can be observed that the CDs were successfully synthesized using biomaterials as a source, which evades the use of large amounts of chemicals which are costly/toxic and complicated post-treatment processes. Ocimum sanctum (also known as Ocimum tenuiflorum, Tulsi), is an aromatic plant in the family lamiaceae and has been used for decades in various medicinal applications for its diverse healing properties [38]. It contains large number of organic compounds like rosmarinic acid, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, carvacrol and many more. Tulsi leaves extract reduce cholesterol, skin disorders, stress, control diabetes and has potential anti-inflammatory properties [38]. In the past years, a number of groups have explored the use of Tulsi leaves for diverse applications [38]. But in our case, we have synthesized CDs from tulsi leaves. Interestingly, the synthesized CDs could not only serve as sensing lead ions, but also act as a contrast agent for live cell imaging.

In the present study, we propose a facile, green, economical, and one-step process of synthesizing CDs through a hydrothermal reaction. Compared with the previous work, this method does not need costly chemicals, high temperature, and strong acid. The synthesized CDs were also applied for the detection of trace Pb2+ ions in real water samples. Comparing with the as proposed fluorescence method for the lead detection, this strategy had several advantages like label-free, simple, rapid, and accurate analysis. The synthesized CDs showed highly selectivity towards Pb2+ ions with limit of detection 0.59nM, which is comparable or even lower than other reported method. We have also demonstrated the practical feasibility of this approach for the detection of Pb2+ ions in cancer cell and real water samples. We further demonstrated that, there is a scope for utilization of the synthesized biocampatable CDs in cell imaging.

The Tulsi leaves (Ocimum sanctum) were collected from Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad campus (India). Aluminium chloride (AlCl3), calcium chloride (CaCl2·6H2O), magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), nickel nitrate (NiNO3)2·6H2O, cobalt nitrate (CoNO3)2·6H2O, copper sulphate (CuSO4)·5H2O, lead sulphate (PbSO4), cadmium sulphate (CdSO4·H2O), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl) stannous chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) was parches from Sigma Aldrich. Ultra pure water (Millipore) system was applied to execute the all experimental works.

The highly fluorescent CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using Tulsi leaves (Ocimum sanctum) as a carbon source. In brief, 10g of fresh Tulsi leaves was mixed with 30mL of distilled water and then transferred in to a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reaction mixture was heated at constant temperature 180°C for 4h until a dark brownish solution was formed. The autoclave was allowed to naturally cool down to room temperature. The obtained dark brownish solution was filtered by a whatman 42 filter paper to eliminate large unreacted particles and filtrates (dark brownish) solution was dialysed by a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1KDa) for 12h and stored at below 4°C for further uses.

The fluorometric detection of Pb2+ ions was carried out at room temperature in distilled water. In this particular experiment, the appropriate quantity of Pb2+ (final concentration 0–10μM) ions was added to 1mg/mL of 2mL of water containing CDs. This solution was stirring for 15min at room temperature and the fluorescence emission spectra was recorded under excitation at 450nm. The selectivity of Pb2+ ions was confirmed by adding of different stock solutions metal ions (Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Na+ Al3+, and K+) instead of Pb2+ ions in similar way.

Three different water sources are used. Pond water collected from local pond, tap water from our laboratory and mineral water purchased from local market. Pond water was filtered twice to remove any solid suspension using ordinary qualitative filter membranes with 0.22μm millipores. Tap water and mineral water was directly used without any pre-treatment.
Water samples were spiked with altered concentrations of Pb+2 ions (5nM, 10nM and 15nM) and added to the sensing system and then resultant fluorescence spectra were measured.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of CDs was performed on Agilent cary 660 instrument. The XRD patterns of CDs were recorded on Phillips PW 1710 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu-Kα radiation. The shape and size of the CDs was examined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Supra 55 with Air Lock chamber for scientific research) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; H-600A, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescent behavior of CDs was recorded by using a Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs were recorded by using (Perkin-Elmer 950, USA). The cell imaging was carried out by fluorescence microscope (Hitachi 7400) for the detection of fluorescence intensity in MDA-MB 468 cells (excitation wave length: 330nm–550nm).

MDA-MB 468 cells were harvested as well as seeded into 96-well plate (90μLwell−1) for overnight. Then, the cells were treated with different concentrations of CDs (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200μgmL−1). After that, the cells were grown for 24h, followed by addition with MTT solution towards each well. After 4h of incubation, the excess culture medium was filtered out, and 150μL of extraction buffer was added. The obtained mixture was incubated for 15min in a dark at 37°C, and its optical density (OD) was recorded by using a microplate reader made by Biotek. The samples were analysed by three times.

MDA-MB 468 cells ware cultured in DMEM medium contained 50 unit/mL penicillin, 50mgmL−1 of streptomycin and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). For in vitro cell imaging studies, MDA-MB 468 cells were seeded in a 35mm plate followed by incubation for 24h in a humidified incubator adjusting conditions of 5% CO2 and 37°C. The cells were then incubated with fresh culture medium containing 100μL of aqueous solution of CDs (1mgmL−1) for 8h. After that, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) by two times and cell imaging was carried out on a fluorescence microscopy. Subsequently, Pb2+ ions (10μM, 2μL) were added and incubated for 30min. Finally, the cells were washed with 1XPBS and imaging experiment were performed under a fluorescence microscopy.

The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using Tulsi leaves as a row material, since Tuisi leaves contains large number of organic molecules (rosmarinic acid, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, carvacrol and many more). These organic molecules converted into CDs by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 4h. The synthesized CDs show strong green emission under ultraviolet light at 365nm as shown in Scheme 1 . The synthesized CDs were applied to the detection of trace amount of Pb2+ ions in real water samples and also can be used for triple negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB 468 cells) imaging. The synthesized CDs were characterised by XRD, TEM, FESEM, EDX, FT-IR, PL and UV–vis spectrophotometer analysis.

The excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of the synthesized CDs were shown in Fig. 1 (a). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs varied with the variation of excitation wavelength from 450nm to 530nm. The maximum emission intensity of CDs were observed at 500nm when excitation wavelength was 450nm may be due to the nature of CDs surface and the maximum number of particles being excited at 450nm. In the UV–vis spectroscopy, there are two absorption peaks one at 280nm (sharp absorption peak) and another around 330nm (broad absorption peak) found as shown in Fig. 1(b). These observed peaks are due to π-π* electronic transition of CC and n-π* electronic transition of CO presence on CDs surface. Inset photograph in Fig. 1(b) represents the aqueous solution of CDs under Visible light (a) and UV light at 365nm (b). The bright green fluorescence of the CDs under UV light is strong enough to be seen by the naked eye, but when lead ions was added, the fluorescent colour was noticeably decreases (c).

The synthesis temperature of CDs was increased from 120°C to 200°C, and the maximum absorption and emission was obtained at 180°C. Further increase in temperature, the absorption and emission intensity was decreased, as shown in Fig.S1 (a and b) respectively. Also, the effects of temperature on the synthesized highly fluorescent CDs under UV light and UV–vis spectrometry are shown in inset of Fig.S1 (a). The water dispersible synthesized CDs were brownish in colour and stable for months as shown in Fig.S2 (a). Among different synthesized time (3h, 4h, 5h, 7h and 9h) of CDs the maximum photoluminescence intensity was observed at 4h, as shown in Fig.S2 (b).

The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated by using quinine sulphate (0.1M H2SO4, QY=0.54) solution as reference together with following formula. The fluorescent quantum yield of CDs in aqueous solution was found to be 9.3% at an excitation wavelength of 450nm shown in Fig.S3. [20]
Q
C D s
=
Q
R
.
I
C D s
/
I
R
.
A
R /
A
C D s
.
η
C D s
2
/
η R
2
Where, “ Q” represents the quantum yield, “ I ” represents the fluorescent intensity, “ A” refers the absorbance measured at exited wavelength, and “ Ƞ ” represents the refractive index of the solvent used. The subscript “ R ” denotes the reference sample and CDs denotes the Carbon Dots.

To determine the analytical sensing of Pb2+ ions, photoluminescence experiments were carried out by the adding CDs towards the 10μM of different metal ions solutions (Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ Co2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Na+, Sn2+ and Al3+) are shown in Fig. 2 (b). The histogram is plotted by taking (F/F0) against equal concentration of the various metal ions. Where, F indicates the fluorescent intensity of CDs in the presence of different metal ions at 450 excitation wavelength and F0 indicates the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence of the metal ions. This histogram shows that the F/F0 ratio is less in presence of Pb2+ ions solution as compared to other metal ions, indicating that the prepared CDs is highly specific and selective towards the Pb2+ ions. High specificity and selectivity towards the Pb2+ ions are probably due to high binding affinity between vacant d-orbital of Pb2+ ions and the amine group present on the surface of CDs than other metal ions. Generally, the nitrogen atom of the amine groups donated electron pair to the vacant d-orbital of lead ions through a nonradiative type electron-transfer are facile compared to the other metal ions and makes surface complexation [18]. The sensing effect of CDs toward different anions, organic molecules and biomolecules are shown in Fig. S7 and S8 .

The continuous decreases of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs with increase the concentration of Pb2+ ions was observed at excitation wavelength of 450nm as shown in Fig. 2(a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs is gradually decreased with increases in concentration of Pb2+ ions (0.01–10μM) as shown in Fig. 2(a). The Pb2+ ions efficiently quenched the fluorescent intensity of CDs approximately about 90% of its initial value, which proved that Pb2+ ions can be easily interact with CDs effectively. The decreases of the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear response with the increasing Pb2+ ions concentration in the range of 0.01–1.0μM. The relative intensity (F/F0) against increasing lead ions concentration shows excellent linearity with a correlation coefficient of R2 =0.998 is shown in Fig.S4. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated from the calibration curve can be depicted as F0/F=75 [Pb2+]+16. Form the calculation it was found that, the limit of detection of Pb2+ ions was approximately 0.59nM, based on 3SD/k (where SD is the standard deviation of the blank signal of the CDs and k is the slope of the calibration curve).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence of CDs in different pH
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, pH effect of the CDs was observed in different buffers solution (pH=2.0–12.0). (The fluorescence intensity of CDs was increased from pH 2.0- pH 7.0 and then decreased from pH 7.0- pH 12.0). The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7.0. These results show that, the pH 7.0 is the most suitable condition for detection of Pb2+ ions. Therefore, we further investigated the fluorescence intensity of various metal ions (Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, K+ and Na+) in aqueous solution, nearly no change in fluorescence intensity of the CDs could be observed except Pb2+ ions. This indicates that these kinds of CDs could be used as highly sensitivity and selectivity probe for Pb2+ ions detection shown in Fig.S5.

Here different concentrations of Pb2+ ions (0.01μM, 0.1μM, 1.0μM and 10μM) were prepared from the stock solution (10μM) and added into the CDs. After one minute, fluorescent photographs of these solutions were taken under UV lamp. The colour of the solution changed from bright green to light green as lead ions concentration was increased. This indicates that the synthesized CDs could be used as highly selectivity and sensitivity analysis for Pb2+ ions detection as shown in Fig. 3 .

The FT- IR spectrum of CDs (Ocimum sanctum) shows strong absorption peaks at 3373cm−1 due to OH group of phenolic group and 3243cm−1 due to the stretching of −NH band of amine groups. The peaks at 2927cm−1 and 2834cm−1 are due to the CH stretching of aliphatic carbon compounds. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1655cm−1 and 1523cm−1 indicate the presence of CO and CC- stretching of aromatic rings respectively. The absorption peaks at 1153cm−1 and 1013cm−1 indicate the presence of CN stretching and ether linkage respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 (a) [39].

The XRD pattern of CDs shows a broad diffraction peak at about 2θ=25° refers to (002) plane as shown in Fig. 4(b). This indicates that CDs are amorphous in nature. The amorphous nature is due to closely packed carbon atoms with alkyl chains [35]. More number of oxygen and nitrogen containing groups on CDs are also evidence of amorphous nature of CDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology analysis and particles size determination
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM analysis was carried out to determine the actual size and shape of the CDs. The HRTEM image clearly shows the synthesized CDs are well spread in water with a spherical shape and fine size distribution of about 3.0nm in diameter shown in Fig. 5 . From FESEM analysis, it is found that the actual shape of the CDs are spherical in nature and the diameters of CDs are between 4 and 7nm, as shown in Fig. 5.The EDX study determined the elemental constitutions of CDs, and the results shows the elemental composition of CDs are C (52.78%), N (9.48%), O (18.30%), Al (19.37%) and Ag (0.07%) are shown in Fig. 5.The Al signal in EDX spectrum can be expected due to the Al foil used during the sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of lead (II) ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the significance and its applicability in real water samples, the ratiometric sensors were applied to detect Pb2+ ions in real water samples. The water samples were spiked with different amounts of Pb2+ ions, including tap water, mineral water and pond water samples. The pond water samples were first filtered to remove any solid suspensions, where as tap water and mineral water samples used directly without any filtrations. In the fluorescence spectra, no Pb2+ ions were detected in the tap water, mineral water and pond water samples. Then, standard addition technique was used. Three levels of 5, 10 and 15nM Pb2+ ions were spiked to 3-fold diluted tap water, mineral water, and pond water samples respectively. For each sample, 3 times repeated analysis were done and the averages were presented with standard deviation. Fig. 6 . shows the comparison of the fluorescence quenching efficiency by Pb2+ ions in different concentrations of the water samples. Clearly, the fluorescence intensity ratio was decreased upon additions of Pb2+ ions for all water samples.

The comparison of the detection limits for Pb2+ ions based on different analytical methods is shown in Table S1. It is clear that the detection limit of our assay protocol is lower than that of the other reported methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CDs through MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the CDs was evaluated in MDA-MB 468 cells by using MTT assay. It is observed from the obtained result that there is very little amount of cell survivability decrease up to 200mgmL−1 of CDs, as shown in Fig. S6. Therefore, these non-toxic CDs are expected as interesting candidate for biomedical applications as well as lead sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MDA-MB 468 cell imaging by synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 proves the internalization of CDs in MDA-MB 468 cells by fluorescent images with the corresponding bright-field images. The CDs treated MDA-MB 468 cells have exhibited bright, blue, green, and red emissions under 330–385nm (ultraviolet), 450–480nm (blue), and 510–550nm (green) excitation wave lengths, respectively. Result revealed that the hydrophilic CDs were spread into the cell membrane, cytoplasmic area and cell nucleus also. When MDA-MB 468 cells were treated with CDs intense fluorescence imaging was observed at different excitation but in presence of Pb2+ (10μM) ions fluorescence intensity suddenly dropped as shown in Fig. 7. This indicates that Pb2+ ions effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of CDs inside the cell. This result demonstrates that the synthesized CDs having good biocompatibility and suitable for detection of Pb2+ ions in living cell [41].

In summary, highly fluorescent CDs have been fabricated by one step hydrothermal method from natural source. Most importantly, no any additional chemical functionalization of CDs is required, which provide the potential opportunities of simplicity and cost efficiency. The obtained CDs exhibit good photostability and have been chosen as a sensing probe for label-free, sensitive detection of Pb2+ ions. The practical application of this probe for Pb2+ ions detection has been successfully demonstrated in real water samples. This fluorescent probes also show good cell-permeability and low cytotoxicity, thus effectively used for the fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB 468 cells. To our knowledge, this is the first reported method to synthesize fluorescent CDs from Ocimum sanctum in a single step and can be applied promisingly in bioimaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the DST, Government of India (SB/FT/CS-068/2013) and Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Carbon
Green synthesis of carbon nanodots as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of mercury (II) ions
J. Nanopart. Res.
Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic, for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Biosens. Bioelectron.
Synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from dried shrimps for cell imaging and boldine drug delivery system
RSC Adv.
A phytopharmacological overview on Ocimum species with special emphasis on Ocimum sanctum
Biomed. Prev. Nutr.
Enhancing antidiabetic and antimicrobial performance of Ocimum basilicum, and Ocimum sanctum (L.) using silver nanoparticles
Saudi J. Bio. Sci.
Imaging of bacterial and fungal cells using fluorescent carbon dots prepared from Carica papaya juice
J. Fluoresc.
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions in cancer cells
Luminescence

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Amit Kumar received his B. Sc. (Chemistry) from Maghadh University, Bodh Gaya (India) in 2007 and M. Sc. (Chemistry) from Patna University, India in 2010. Currently he is pursuing Ph. D in chemistry at Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, under the supervision of Dr. Sumanta Kumar Sahu. His research interests focused on the synthesis of carbon dots based sensors and bio imaging.

Angshuman Ray Chowdhuri received his B. Sc. (Chemistry) from The University of Burdwan, India in 2011, and M. Sc. (Chemistry) from Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, India in 2013. He is currently working as a senior research fellow at Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, India under the supervision of Dr. Sumanta Kumar Sahu. His research interests include the development of smart nanoscale materials and its applications in anticancer drug delivery, sensing/biosensing.

​Dr. Dipranjan Laha received his B.Sc in Biotechnology from Vidyasagar University, India (2009), and M.Sc Biotechnology from Berhampur University, India (2011). He has completed his Ph.D under the supervision of Prof. Parimal Karmakr, Dept. of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, India and Prof. Panchanan Prmanik, Dept. of Chemistry, Indian Institute Technology, Kharagpur India. He is currently working as a post-doctoral fellow under the supervision of Dr. Kuladip Jana, Bose Institute, Kolkata, India. His research interests include the targeted drug delivery in breast cancer cells.

Triveni Kumar Mahto, Ph D (pursuing), finished his M. Phil. in Applied Chemistry in 2009 from Indian School of Mines Dhanbad, India. Before M. Phil, he completed his M. Sc. and B. Ed. from Vinoba Bhave University, Hazaribag (India). Currently he is a lecturer in the Engineering Chemistry Department at Birsa Institute of Technology Sindri, Dhanbad (India). His research interests are focused on energy and environment: design and development of nanomaterials working against environmental pollution, biofuel generation, development of new strategies for alternating energy etc.

Prof. Parimal Karmakar received his PhD in DNA damage and repair in human cell from Calcutta University, Kolkata, India in 1994. His research interests include cell biology, molecular biology, nanobiotechnology. He has published several papers in different reputed journal. He is currently working as a Professor at the Dept. of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, India.

Dr. Sumanta K. Sahu received his Ph.D in nano-medicine from Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur in 2011. He is currently the head of the research group ‘Functional Nanomaterials for biomedical applications’ at the Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad since 2012. He has co-authored over 40 scientific research papers. His research interests include nanomaterials, functional porous materials, and their applications in drug delivery, catalysis, sensing, and separation.
